hub_subtitle: Conversação para Feiras Internacionais de Turismo & Hotelaria

lesson.menu_title: Back in the Game -- Your Professional Identity
lesson.menu_desc: Describing your role and current projects at the agency -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 2 -- Back in the Game
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Andrea Aggio | Aula 2 -- Back in the Game

listenings.text: Hi Andrea, it is Marco from Rome. It was great to see you again at the agency last month. I am calling with some news. At the moment, our company is growing fast, and we are opening three new hotels in the south of Italy this season. I know you manage the commercial team at Escape Turismo and that you handle the European itineraries, so I think there is a real opportunity here. Right now, we are looking for a strong partner for our incoming tourism, and you are the first person I thought of. Please give me a call this week, and we can talk about how our teams could work together. Take care, and talk soon.

Good afternoon, team, and thank you for joining this short briefing. I want to give you a quick picture of where we are right now. Our travel agency is having a very busy season. At the moment, we are expanding our hotel portfolio in southern Europe, and we are also launching a new outbound travel program for corporate clients. As you know, our commercial team usually works with partners in about ten countries, but this year we are adding four more. Please remember that each of you is responsible for one region, so keep your notes up to date. We are meeting the new suppliers next week, and I am counting on all of you to make a great impression.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: to organize people and tasks so they work together
to be in charge of a team or an area
the group of hotels a company works with
to deal with a task, a client, or a problem
visitors who come into your country
to watch over work and make sure it is done well
a company that plans and sells trips to clients
to stay in contact and work closely with someone

reading.rtitle: A Day in the Life of a Travel Director
paras: Andrea works for a travel agency, and her days are never quiet. As Commercial Director, she manages a team of eight people and oversees every deal the agency signs. She is responsible for the European itineraries, so she liaises with hoteliers, coordinates the calendar, and handles the biggest clients herself. Most weeks look the same: she reads reports in the morning, calls partners in the afternoon, and answers dozens of emails in between.

This season, however, is different. Right now, the agency is going through a period of fast growth. At the moment, Andrea is expanding the hotel portfolio in southern Europe, and her team is launching a new outbound travel program for corporate clients. She is also preparing for two international fairs, where she is meeting suppliers she has known for years.

People often ask her what makes a good travel professional. Her answer is simple. 'In this job, you manage details and you build relationships at the same time,' she says. 'The routine keeps the agency running, but the new projects keep it alive. Today, incoming tourism to Europe is booming, and we are working hard to be ready for it.'
reading.source: Adapted from a tourism careers feature, The Travel Professional

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Andrea's job is only about answering emails and reading reports every day.
A good travel director keeps the daily routine running while leading new projects at the same time.
The travel agency is closing down because the season is too busy.

items: Andrea manages a team and oversees the agency's deals.
The text says she manages a team of eight and oversees every deal the agency signs.
Every season at the agency looks exactly the same for Andrea.
The text says this season is different -- the agency is going through fast growth.
Right now, the team is launching a new outbound travel program for corporate clients.
The second paragraph states this directly.
Andrea believes the routine alone is enough to keep the agency alive.
She says the routine keeps it running, but the new projects keep it alive.

parts: every deal the agency signs. This season, the agency is going through fast growth: at the moment, the team is
the hotel portfolio in southern Europe. Andrea also

gapfill.text: Notice: we use the present simple for permanent facts and routines (she manages, she oversees), and the present continuous for temporary projects happening now (the team is expanding, we are launching). The words 'right now', 'at the moment' and 'this season' are signals for the continuous.

lf.text: Notice: sentences A and C describe permanent facts and routines, so they use the present simple (manage, works). Sentences B and D describe temporary things happening around now, so they use the present continuous (are expanding, am working). The time words tell you which one to choose.

bank.label: Useful language for describing your role and projects
items: I am the Commercial Director, and I manage...
In a typical week, I usually...
Right now, we are working on...
At the moment, our team is expanding...
Part of my job is to liaise with...
I oversee the... and coordinate the...
A new contact asks about your job. Describe your role using the present simple: what you manage, oversee, and are responsible for.
The same contact asks what you are working on this season. Describe two current projects using the present continuous ('right now...', 'at the moment...').
Describe a typical week at the agency: three things you usually do (present simple) and one thing that is different this month (present continuous).
Explain the difference between incoming tourism and outbound travel for your agency, and say which one your team is focusing on right now.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = manages, 2 = oversees, 3 = expanding, 4 = liaises, 5 = handles.
Model role (simple): 'I am the Commercial Director. I manage the commercial team and I am responsible for the European itineraries.'
Model project (continuous): 'Right now, we are expanding our hotel portfolio, and this month I am working on a new partnership in Italy.'
Simple vs continuous: use the simple for permanent facts and routines; use the continuous for temporary things happening around now.
answerkey.note: Stative verbs like know, want, and be are not used in the continuous: say 'I know many hoteliers', not 'I am knowing'.
